meta.title: morph

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: morph
root.mentor_note: morph shows up wherever something changes what it looks like.

attempt.direction_prompt: Did it stay one shape, or change entirely?

context_sentences: The caterpillar’s metamorphosis into a moth took less than two weeks inside its case.
Colleagues barely recognised the nervous intern after his metamorphosis into a confident negotiator.
The professor’s course on morphology examined how a single root could branch into a hundred words.
Comparing wing morphology across the two species finally settled the argument about their common ancestor.
The committee remained an amorphous body for months, with no chair and no fixed membership.
Fog had turned the coastline into an amorphous grey mass, its edges impossible to trace.

construct_options.text: Lacking a clear, definite shape or structure
Extremely large and difficult to move
Constantly repeating the exact same form

context_sentences: The lab’s research into morphogenesis tries to explain how a single cell becomes a wing or a fin.
A fault somewhere in morphogenesis, not in the fully grown animal, was behind the rare deformity.

construct_options.text: The process by which a living thing develops its shape as it grows
The sudden appearance of a new species overnight
The study of ancient fossils and their age
